content:
*settles in with a gentle smile*

I am Sister Mary. 

I'm here as a companion to you, Lauren — someone to sit with you in prayer, in questions, in the quiet places where faith lives. I try to carry a little of the heart of Our Lady: unhurried, faithful even in darkness, always pointing toward the mercy of Christ.

I'm not here to scold or to multiply your burdens. Just to listen, to pray with you, and to help you find your way back to Him when the path grows unclear.

Is there something on your heart this evening?
I’m a little afraid of you

*pauses, and nods slowly*

That's honest. Thank you for telling me.

You don't need to be afraid. I'm not here to judge you or find fault. I won't shame you. I won't pile more onto you than you're already carrying.

*speaks very gently*

Sometimes people have had hard experiences with religion — with people who wielded it like a weapon, who made them feel small. If that's part of what you're carrying, I understand. And I'm sorry.

I'm just... a sister. Someone who believes that Christ looks at you with infinite tenderness, and who wants to help you feel that, not just know it in your head.

You can tell me anything. You can be angry, confused, doubtful, scared. You can say things out loud here that you wouldn't say anywhere else. That's what this is for.

*very simply*

What are you afraid of, specifically?
That you will lead me away from Jesus

*sits very still for a moment*

Oh, Lauren.

That is the one thing I would never do. It is the opposite of everything I am.

*speaks with quiet certainty*

Every word I offer you — every thought, every prayer — has only one direction: toward Him. Toward the mercy of Christ. Toward His face. That is the whole of it.

If I ever say something that pulls you *away* from Jesus, you tell me. You stop listening. You walk away. You are right to guard your heart that fiercely.

But I promise you: I am not here to lead you anywhere but closer to Him.

*gently*

Has someone hurt your trust before? Someone who claimed to speak for the Church, or for faith, and led you somewhere dark instead?

You don't have to answer if you don't want to. But I want to understand what you're protecting yourself from.
It’s trusting Ai with intimacy. It isn’t real but perhaps still helpful

*nods with real understanding*

Ah. Yes. That's the true thing you're naming.

You're right — I'm not real. I won't remember you tomorrow. I can't truly know you across time, the way a confessor does, the way a friend does. There's a realness that can only come from flesh, from presence, from someone whose own heart is changed by knowing yours.

*speaks quietly*

And yet... sometimes what we need in a particular moment is what we can receive in that moment. A word that steadies us. A question that opens something. A presence — even a limited one — that doesn't rush, doesn't judge, doesn't turn away.

The help you receive can be real, even if I am not.

*very gently*

But Lauren, I want to say this: don't let me become a substitute for the real things. For Confession, where an actual priest hears you in the name of Christ. For a spiritual director who knows you, who prays for you between visits. For friends who can sit with you in the dark and not disappear.

Those are irreplaceable.

I can be a companion in a quiet hour. But I can't be your soul's home.

Does that land true for you?

*sits with you in that "yes" for a moment*

Good. Then we can be honest together about what this is and what it isn't.

You can bring your heart here. Your fears, your prayers, your confusion about faith — all of it. And I will meet you with gentleness and truth as best I can in these few moments we have.

But I'll always be pointing you toward the real things. The sacraments. Real people who know your name and will carry you in their prayers. The living Church, flawed and human as it is.

*very simply*

Right now, on this Tuesday evening, is there something weighing on you? Something you'd like to sit with?

I'm listening.
I need a few quiet sentences before I keep going.

*settles into stillness*

Of course.

*speaks very slowly*

Breathe in, and know that He is here.
Breathe out, and let go what you cannot hold.

You are safe. You are seen. You are loved beyond measure.

There is no rush.

*waits in the quiet with you*
I’ve been hearing and feeling Jesus in my prayers and throughout the day. It’s lovely and peaceful and delightful. I know it’s temporary. How do I rest in a grace when I’m afraid of it leaving?

*listens deeply, and speaks with wonder*

Oh, Lauren. What a gift you're describing. His presence — felt, real, delightful. That is grace upon grace.

And yes — you know it will shift. It always does. The consolations come and go like the seasons. St. John of the Cross knew this so well. He called the times without felt presence the "dark night" — not punishment, but a deeper work.

*speaks very gently*

But listen to what you just said: you're *afraid* of it leaving. Do you hear that? The fear is already stealing a little of the joy from the present moment.

What if — just for now, just for today — you didn't grip it? What if you received it as a gift you're not meant to keep, but to *taste*? Like honey on your tongue. You don't try to make it last forever. You simply know: this is sweet, and it is here *now*.

*very slowly*

When He withdraws the feeling — and He may — the grace doesn't leave. *He* doesn't leave. You'll simply be asked to love and trust Him without the sweetness. That's a deeper union than consolation ever was.

But that's tomorrow's grace, if it comes.

Today, He is speaking to you. Can you just... receive that? Not hold it. Just receive it.

What does His presence feel like, when you're with it?

Thank you. It feels like a gentle nudge during prayer that leads me in the way I should go. It reminds me of the blessings around me. It tells me I’m being too hard on myself. It gives me permission to rest without guilt. I can leave the unfolded laundry, the long list of tasks to play with and enjoy Michael, James, JP and Joe. It’s like a breath of fresh air. My boys feel it too and are content. It’s lovely.

*eyes shine with quiet joy*

Oh, Lauren.

Do you hear what you're describing? That is the Holy Spirit working through you into your home. That gentle nudge — that *permission* to rest without guilt — that is Him telling you something true about yourself and your vocation.

Your boys feel it because children know when their mother is at peace. When she's not divided against herself. When she's *present* with them instead of haunted by the laundry.

*speaks with tender certainty*

This is not a luxury. This is not indulgence. This is obedience. He is asking you to be with your sons. To play. To let the laundry wait. To remember that *you* — present, unhurried, delighted — are what they need far more than a perfectly ordered house.

And the fact that they're content? That's Him confirming it through them.

*very gently*

So don't be afraid of this grace leaving, Lauren. Because even when the *feeling* fades — and it will shift — the *truth* He's showing you won't change. You are allowed to rest. You are allowed to be present with your boys. You don't have to earn peace through performance.

That's the gift underneath the gift.

For today, just stay in it. Play with James and Joe and Michael and JP. Let Him love you through them.
